title: Planning AC servicing? Wait a bit. IMD sees cooler April, but issues May–June heatwave warning for these s - The Economic Times

full_text:
Across many parts of India, especially in April, maximum daytime temperatures are likely to remain normal or even below normal. This is largely due to the influence of frequent western disturbances, which are expected to bring rainfall and keep temperatures in check, particularly in northwest regions.
Heatwaves Still Loom in Several States
However, this does not mean the summer will be mild throughout. The IMD has warned that several regions, including Rajasthan, Gujarat, Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, and parts of peninsular India, may experience above-normal heatwave days between April and June.Eastern, northeastern, and coastal regions are particularly likely to face extended heatwave spells, lasting between two to eight days. In cities like Delhi-NCR, the most intense heat is expected to arrive later, primarily during May and June rather than April.
A heatwave is officially declared when temperatures reach at least 40°C in plains and 30°C in hilly regions. Typically, India sees three to five heatwave days during this period, but this year, some regions may exceed that average.
Warmer Nights Could Be the Real Discomfort
Interestingly, while daytime temperatures may not spike dramatically in many areas, nights are expected to be warmer than usual. The IMD predicts above-normal minimum temperatures across most parts of the country, which could make conditions feel more uncomfortable, especially without proper cooling.This means that even if daytime heat feels manageable, the lack of nighttime relief could still push people towards using air conditioning more frequently later in the season.
Why You Can Delay AC Repairs, for Now
Given the relatively moderate conditions expected in April, this could be a good opportunity to postpone immediate AC repairs or servicing. You may not need heavy cooling just yet, particularly in northern and central parts of India where rainfall and cloud cover could keep temperatures down.Delaying repairs could also help you avoid peak-season rush and higher service costs, which typically rise sharply in May and June when demand surges.
Weather Patterns Behind the Shift
The current outlook is influenced by neutral El Niño–Southern Oscillation (ENSO) conditions, which are expected to continue through April to June. However, there is a growing likelihood of El Niño developing later, which could impact the latter part of the monsoon season.Additionally, March saw an unusually high number of western disturbances, eight instead of the usual five to six, leading to widespread rainfall, thunderstorms, and even hailstorms in some regions. This activity helped lower temperatures across much of the country.
Plan Smart, Stay Prepared
While the early summer may offer some relief, the heat is far from gone. If you’re considering AC repairs, you can afford to wait briefly, but not indefinitely. The real test of summer is likely to arrive in May and June.Use this window wisely: monitor the weather, plan ahead, and schedule servicing before the peak heat sets in. That way, you stay comfortable without the last-minute scramble.
